verdict_reasoning: EREG (epiregulin) is a single-pass transmembrane growth factor ligand of the EGF receptor family. The canonical biology is: (1) synthesized as a ~150 aa TM proligand with an N-terminal signal peptide, extracellular EGF-like domain, TM anchor, and short cytoplasmic tail; (2) undergoes regulated ectodomain shedding by ADAM sheddases (ADAM10, ADAM17), releasing a soluble ~43 aa mature ligand into the extracellular space that drives paracrine/autocrine EGFR signaling; (3) the TM precursor stage is short-lived and the soluble shed form is the canonical biological actor in most contexts. The protein does transit the plasma membrane as a TM-anchored form before proteolytic release, but the membrane-anchored stage is transient and not the dominant surface-accessible biological entity. Clinical EGFR-targeted programs (cetuximab, trastuzumab, etc.) target EGFR itself, not EREG; anti-EREG antibodies would target the predominantly soluble shed ligand, not a stable surface pool. No documented dual-localization cycling, no cancer/cell-death ecto-restricted display, no tissue-restricted surface lineage expression. The transient TM precursor qualifies as `dual_localization` rather than `yes` because the soluble shed form dominates the biology.
